coredata.dc:title: Selective fluorometric determination of microcystin-LR using a segment template molecularly imprinted by polymer-capped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Zhenke
Lu, Ruiqin
Wang, Shiyu
Xiang, Cheng
Xie, Chuangjie
Zheng, Manni
Tian, Xingguo
Xu, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: Microcystin-LR (MC-LR) is a virulent hepatotoxin that is pervasive in rivers and lakes. The contamination of water with MC-LR poses a significant threat to human life and the water ecology. Therefore, it is of great interest developing a reliable, quick, and sensitive method for the detection of MC-LR. Herein, a novel type of molecularly imprinted polymer-coated carbon quantum dot (MIP@CQDs@SiO2) fluorescence sensor is developed for the selective and sensitive determination of MC-LR. The MIP@CQDs@SiO2 fluorescence sensor was fabricated via sol–gel polymerization using L-leucine as the segment template molecule, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the signal materials, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane as the monomer, and tetraethylorthosilicate as the crosslinker. The specific recognition sites in the polymer layers could selectively adsorb MC-LR molecules, which caused fluorescence-quenching behavior of CQDs via an electron transfer process. Under optimal conditions, the linear response associated with MC-LR was 1–1000 μg/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0093 μg/L. The imprinting factor was 5.0, and the adsorption ability was 3.23 mg/g. The proposed method was proved to efficiently detect MC-LR in real water samples. In addition, the fluorescence sensor proposed in this work shows good stability in a complex matrix, excellent selectivity and sensitivity to MC-LR in real sample analysis, and remarkable potential in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymer
Carbon quantum dot
Fluorescence sensor
Sol–gel polymerization
MC-LR

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and the fluorescence sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31501554 ), Guangdong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( 2020A1515010517 ), and Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Planning Project of China ( 2017A020208059 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation processes for CQDs and MIP@SiO2@CQDs.

TEM images of CQDs (A), MIP@SiO2@CQDs (B), and NIP@SiO2@CQDs (C). The insets show size distribution (top left), images of photoilluminated solutions under visible light and 360-nm UV light, and lattice fringes (bottom right).

Fluorescence intensity of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 (A) and NIP@CQDs@SiO2 (B) excited at 360 nm after recombination with different concentrations of MC-LR (0, 1, 25, 50, 75, 100, 250, 500, 750, and 1000 μg/L from top to bottom). The insets correspond to the Stern–Volmer plots. The error bars represent the standard deviation of the three measurements.
Selectivity of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 and NIP@CQDs@SiO2 (CMC-LR = CMC-RR = CMC-YR = 100 μg/L).
Quantitative determination of MC-LR in water samples with the method of this work (n = 3).
Comparison of analytical methods or products for the determination of MC-LR in water samples.

head.ce:title: Selective fluorometric determination of microcystin-LR using a segment template molecularly imprinted by polymer-capped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microcystin-LR (MC-LR) is a virulent hepatotoxin that is pervasive in rivers and lakes. The contamination of water with MC-LR poses a significant threat to human life and the water ecology. Therefore, it is of great interest developing a reliable, quick, and sensitive method for the detection of MC-LR. Herein, a novel type of molecularly imprinted polymer-coated carbon quantum dot (MIP@CQDs@SiO2) fluorescence sensor is developed for the selective and sensitive determination of MC-LR. The MIP@CQDs@SiO2 fluorescence sensor was fabricated via sol–gel polymerization using L-leucine as the segment template molecule, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the signal materials, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane as the monomer, and tetraethylorthosilicate as the crosslinker. The specific recognition sites in the polymer layers could selectively adsorb MC-LR molecules, which caused fluorescence-quenching behavior of CQDs via an electron transfer process. Under optimal conditions, the linear response associated with MC-LR was 1–1000 μg/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0093 μg/L. The imprinting factor was 5.0, and the adsorption ability was 3.23 mg/g. The proposed method was proved to efficiently detect MC-LR in real water samples. In addition, the fluorescence sensor proposed in this work shows good stability in a complex matrix, excellent selectivity and sensitivity to MC-LR in real sample analysis, and remarkable potential in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Microcystin-LR (MC-LR), widely found in contaminated rivers, lakes, and reservoirs, is considered the most acute type of hepatotoxin produced by cyanobacterial blooms. According to a report [1], MC-LR can negatively affect aquatic organisms, safety of drinking water, and human health. If one mistakenly drinks water contaminated with MC-LR, it can potentially cause acute gastroenteritis and even liver cancer [2]. Reportedly, hundreds of elephants died suddenly in northwest Botswana in 2020. Investigation and verification revealed that the elephants had drunk water contaminated with excessive amounts of microcystin. Therefore, the World Health Organization has established the maximum limit of MC-LR in drinking water as 1 μg/L [3]. Consequently, the demand for methods for the selective and sensitive determination of MC-LR in polluted water has drastically increased in recent years.

Currently available analytical methods for detecting microcystin mainly include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4–7], cytotoxicity detection technology [8], immunoassay tests [9–13], and various sensing methods [14–16]. Among these methods, HPLC and immunoassay tests are widely used for MC-LR detection in polluted water. Though both these methods theoretically exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity to MC-LR, their practical applicability is limited owing to the requirement of large and expensive equipment and long-term-trained professional staff [17,18]. Considering the abovementioned problems, an innovative and low-cost fluorescence-sensing method [19–23] that couples molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) with fluorescence materials is being widely applied to the selective and sensitive detection of hazardous toxins. The practicality and applicability of this innovative method have resulted in various achievements for the detection of hazardous toxins. Regardless, only some studies have investigated MC-LR detection using fluorescence sensors, and the development of a novel probe to alleviate the increasingly urgent demand in recent years is of great interest.

Molecular imprinting is a technique used to prepare highly crosslinked copolymerization materials [24–26] in a convenient and low-cost way. MIP can be prepared through different methods such as bulk polymerization, in situ polymerization, precipitation polymerization, suspension polymerization, swelling polymerization, and sol–gel polymerization [27]. The prepared MIP exhibit unparalleled selectivity for the imprinted analytes in a complex matrix because the recognition sites with respect to size, shape, and functional group are customized for the template molecules [28]. Besides, MIP possess excellent properties such as high surface area, remarkable physical robustness, and good chemical stability compared with biological systems [29]. Therefore, to realize high selectivity for analytes, in recent years, molecularly imprinting has been extensively applied to the preparation of novel nanosensors, including electrochemical sensor [30–33], voltammetric sensor [34], and quartz crystal microbalance sensor [35]. Moreover, typical fluorescent materials involve organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots. Nevertheless, the fluorescence properties of the abovementioned materials are generally negatively influenced by problems of cytotoxicity and photobleaching. By contrast, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [36–41] possess advantages such as low cost, environmental friendliness, nontoxicity, easy preparation, and excellent optical properties, including unique photoluminescence (PL) properties, high quantum yield, chemical inertness, and strong photostability. Therefore, CQDs exhibit absolute superiority as fluorescent materials in the preparation of fluorescence sensors.

In this study, we report an innovative MIP-coated CQDs (MIP@CQDs@SiO2) fluorescence sensor. MC-LR is not only an expensive analyte but also a perilous toxin with high risk of leakage [42]. Therefore, it cannot be used as a template in MIP preparation. Fortunately, Song et al. [43] successfully fabricated MIP using L-arginine, which is a part of microcystin, as the segment template for the adsorption and determination of microcystin, thereby developing a new methodology that considerably influenced our study. After studying a series of structural analogs of MC-LR, we selected L-leucine as the segment template to prepare MIP. The fluorescence sensor was fabricated via a sol–gel polymerization process using L-leucine as the segment template, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) as the monomer, and tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) as the crosslinker. The sol–gel polymerization method was adopted because of its easy fabrication process, ecofriendly synthesis, and mild polymerization. The fluorescence sensor could be effectively quenched via photoinduced electron transfer after MC-LR adsorption. The prepared MIP@CQDs@SiO2 fluorescence sensor was well characterized, and the recognition and quenching properties were explicitly tested. The proposed fluorescence-sensing method was successfully established and proved to be applicable to the selective and sensitive detection of MC-LR in trace quantification in real water samples.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Talos F200S microscope with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra ranging from 450 to 4000 cm−1 in KBr were measured with an FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Adsorption spectra were acquired using a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and the fluorescence spectra were measured using a 970CRT spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China).

Tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), L-leucine, petroleum ether, anhydrous citric acid, 3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyldimethoxymethylsilane (AEAPMS), N,N-Dimethylformamide(DMF), NH3·H2O (25%), anhydrous methanol, and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The standard analytes, i.e., MC-LR, microcystin-RR (MC-RR), and microcystin-YR (MC-YR), were purchased from Beijing Puhuaren Technology Development Co., Ltd (Beijing, China).

The CQDs were synthesized via the pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid and surface passivation of AEAPMS according to a previously reported method that was slightly modified [44]. First, 10 mL AEAPMS was inserted into a three-necked flask, and the reaction system was degassed with nitrogen for 10 min and heated up to 200 °C. Second, 0.5-g anhydrous citric acid dissolved in 3 mL anhydrous ethanol was quickly injected into the reaction system under vigorous stirring at 200 °C for 1 min. Finally, the prepared crude products were centrifuged at 12000 rpm to eliminate the unreacted substances, and the obtained supernatants were filtered through a 0.22-μm filter membrane and washed thrice with petroleum ether. Then, 1 mL as-purified product was dispersed in 9 mL anhydrous ethanol solution and stored at 4 °C in darkness before use.

The fluorescence sensor (MIP@CQDs@SiO2) was prepared via the sol–gel polymerization process [45] on the surface of the CQDs. First, 15-mg L-leucine, 80 μL APTES, and 10 mL DMF were added into a 25-mL beaker and combined. Second, 1 mL as-prepared CQDs were added into the reaction system after stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, 140 μL TEOS and 100 μL NH3·H2O (25%) were added dropwise into the mixture while stirring, and the reaction system was sealed and stirred in darkness for 16 h. Finally, the obtained crude products were centrifuged and washed with methanol several times to eliminate all the unreacted L-leucine and stored at 4 °C prior to use. The NIP@CQDs@SiO2 was prepared in a similar manner without the addition of L-leucine.

All fluorescence intensity measurements were performed under identical conditions. The excitation and emission slit widths of the fluorescence spectrophotometer were set to 10 nm. First, 300 μL MIP@CQDs@SiO2 with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL and 100 μL MC-LR solution with a concentration of 1–1000 μg/L were added into a 2-mL standard cuvette under optimal conditions. After incubation for a certain time in a shaking table, the fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm in an emission wavelength range of 400–650 nm. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were obtained to evaluate the adsorption and quenching properties of MIP@CQDs@SiO2. Fluorescence measurement with NIP@CQDs@SiO2 was also conducted using the same procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and the fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation processes for the CQDs and MIP@CQDs@SiO2 fluorescence sensor are displayed in Fig. 1 . The CQDs were fabricated via the decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid and surface passivation of AEAPMS. Anhydrous citric acid was pyrolyzed into the principal part of CQDs comprising carboxyl and hydroxyl groups under a degassed and heated atmosphere. Amino groups were successfully grafted on the principal part of the CQDs after the surface passivation of AEAPMS. The MIP@CQDs@SiO2 fluorescence sensor was synthesized at the surface of the CQDs via sol–gel polymerization, in which CQDs were integrated into the MIP through silicon-containing groups including Si–O–Si, Si–O, and Si–O–C to form a three-dimensional network crosslinked structure. The MIP were obtained using L-leucine as the segment template, APTES as the functional monomer, TEOS as the crosslinker, and NH3·H2O (25%) as the catalyst. Then, the CQDs were grafted on the MIP through silicon-containing groups, and the MIP@CQDs@SiO2 fluorescence sensor was obtained by eliminating L-leucine via centrifugation and washing. Figure S1 presents the fracture and reconnection of the hydrogen bonds corresponding to the washing of L-leucine and the recombination of MC-LR. Theoretically, the structures of L-leucine and APTES showed that the electronegativity of the O atom from the COOH group of L-leucine was stronger than that of N from NH2 of APTES, indicating that the electron cloud density obtained using the O atoms was larger than that obtained using the N atoms. This can be considered responsible for the preferable formation of the hydrogen bonds of N–H---O structure compared with those of C = O---H. Moreover, the H–N---H hydrogen bonds could be generated from the two NH2 groups of L-leucine and APTES. Therefore, every mol of L-leucine formed hydrogen bonds with 3 mol of APTES, providing three spatial recognition sites after the elimination of L-leucine.

The size and morphology of the CQDs and MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2 were characterized via TEM. From Fig. 2 (A), the diameters of the CQDs, which mostly exhibited uniform distribution and size, were approximately 2–4 nm. Furthermore, the average particle size of the CQDs dispersed in anhydrous ethanol was estimated to be 3 ± 0.5 nm using Nano Measurer software. In addition, the CQDs had obvious uniform lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm. The CQDs emitted bright blue visible light under 365-nm UV light, whereas they formed a colorless and transparent solution under daylight, indicating their successful synthesis. In the case of MIP@CQDs@SiO2, TEOS acted as the imprinted polymeric material used to connect the CQDs and MIP, providing structural integrity and stability. As shown in Fig. 2(B) and (C), MIP@CQDs@SiO2 and NIP@CQDs@SiO2 exhibited a highly crosslinked morphology, and their average diameters were approximately 250 and 200 nm, respectively, indicating the successful preparation of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 and NIP@CQDs@SiO2.

FT-IR spectroscopy measurements were performed to elucidate the specific functional groups of the CQDs. As shown in Figure S2, the typical signals at 1597 cm−1 (δ) and 3290 and 3373 cm−1 (υ) can be attributed to the –NH–apparently originating from AEAPMS and that at 1650 cm−1 (υ) can be attributed to the C = O stretching vibrations. Moreover, the signal at 3300 cm−1 (υ) can be attributed to O–H stretching, the bands at 980–1200 cm−1 (υ) can be attributed to the Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching vibrations and Si–O stretching vibrations, and the signal at 796 cm−1 (δ) represents the Si–CH2 bending vibrations. In addition, the CH stretching vibrations (υ, 2800–3000 cm−1) and CH2 bending vibrations (δ, 1462 cm−1) could be separately observed in the spectra. The FT-IR spectrum of the eluted MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2 was similar to that of the CQDs except for the enlargement of the adsorption peak width of Si–O–Si and Si–O from 980 to 1200 cm−1 (υ) to 800–1350 cm−1 (υ) and the appearance of a Si–O–C band at 454 cm−1 (δasym), which illustrates the addition of TEOS and its crosslinking ability in the structure. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 and NIP@CQDs@SiO2 were similar, confirming the complete elution of L-leucine.

The optical properties of the CQDs were investigated via UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Figure S3, under irradiation with a 365-nm UV lamp, the UV–vis spectra of the CQDs showed characteristic adsorption peaks at 222 and 356 nm, which can be attributed to the π–π* transition of the C = C bonds and the n–π* transition of the C = O bonds, respectively. The C = C bonds may originate from the pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid that caused intramolecular dehydration. Based on Figure S3, the emission wavelengths and fluorescence intensity are observed to vary with the excitation wavelength, proving that the CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission. The CQDs emission peak was generally narrow and symmetrical under excitation wavelengths of 320 to 400 nm with increments of 10 nm, showing a trend of first increasing, then decreasing, and gradually red-shifting. Additionally, the CQDs resulted in the strongest fluorescence intensity and a full width at half maximum as small as 50 nm when excited at 360 nm, illustrating that the as-prepared CQDs showed excellent fluorescence ability.

Because quantum yield (QY) is a key property with respect to the emission efficiency of the CQDs, the relative QY of the CQDs was measured using a previously reported method [46]. The principle of this method is that the fluorescent emission intensity region of quinine sulfate is similar to that of the CQDs excited at 360 nm. First, the adsorption of the CQDs solution was measured in a range of 0.01–0.05 a.u. with increments of 0.01 a.u. in case of the 360-nm UV light to avoid the concentration quenching effect and self-adsorption phenomena. Then, by comparing the integrated fluorescent emission region of quinine sulfate and the CQDs, the relative QY of the latter can be obtained as follows:
(1)
( Q Y )
C
=
( Q Y )
Q
K C
/
K Q
(
η C
/
η Q
)
2
,
where (QY)C and (QY)Q are the QYs of CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively, (KC/KQ) is the slope ratio of the CQDs and quinine sulfate determined by the curves given in Figure S4, and (ηC/ηQ) is the solvent refractive index ratio of the CQDs and quinine sulfate. The refractive index of anhydrous alcohol in the case of the CQDs is 1.36, whereas that of 0.1 M sulfuric acid for quinine sulfate is 1.33. Using the above equation, the QY was estimated to be 51%, indicating that the CQDs exhibited strong fluorescent emission efficiency. This may be attributed to the surface passivation of AEAPMS filling the surface defects of the CQDs. Subsequently, the electrons captured by the surface defects of the CQDs could return to the valence band from the conduction band via radiative transition, forming electron–hole pairs with holes in the valence band and emitting more photons. Consequently, the QY of the CQDs considerably increased.

Selecting the appropriate ratio of the segment template, monomer, and crosslinker was very important during the preparation of the MIP@CQDs@SiO2 fluorescence sensor [47]. If the amounts of monomers were considerably low, they failed to fully interact with the template. Otherwise, nonspecific adsorption could be easily realized because of the self-polymerization of the monomers. In an experiment, 15-mg (0.11 mol) L-leucine was combined with APTES in increments of 20 μL (40–160 μL, 0.18–0.72 mol). NIP@CQDs@SiO2 was prepared similarly but without L-leucine. The imprinted factors that revealed the quenching effect of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 were obtained based on the ratio of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 and NIP@CQDs@SiO2, the curve top of which represented the strongest quenching effect. As shown in Figure S5(A), the imprinted factors increased first and then decreased after reaching the maximum value at 80 μL. Similarly, an excess of crosslinkers rendered the polymers inflexible and adversely affected the mass transfer effect. By contrast, a considerably low amount of crosslinkers caused the polymers to exhibit weak mechanical strength. According to Figure S5(B), the optimal amount of TEOS was 140 μL. Therefore, the optimal quenching effect of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 was achieved with a molar ratio of 1:3.2:6.1 for L-leucine, APTES, and TEOS. Another crucial factor was the polymerization time. A very short polymerization time resulted in MIP@CQDs@SiO2 with a fragile structure and insufficient recognition sites. As shown in Figure S5(C), the quenching effect of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 was optimal for a polymerization time of 16 h.

The most important factors governing the adsorption capacity of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 were solvent pH and incubation time. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching effect was monitored by changing both these factors. As depicted in Figure S6, the fluorescence quenching effect was gradually promoted in a pH range of 3–13, reaching the maximum between 11 and 13, indicating that the PL ability of the CQDs was dependent on pH. The PL ability of the CQDs considerably decreased when pH was reduced from 6 to 3, whereas it improved significantly when pH was 8–13, indicating that MIP@CQDs@SiO2 achieved the best fluorescence quenching effect in an alkaline medium. This is most probably due to the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of the CQDs [48,49]. Some of the surface functional groups of the CQDs, including –OH, –COOH, and –NH2, probably caused a pH-induced protonation effect in an acidic environment, resulting in the reduced PL ability of the actual emissive moiety coupled with the surface functional groups. In contrast, the PL ability of the actual emissive moiety increased in an alkaline environment, which can be attributed to the deprotonation of some surface functional groups. Therefore, the PL intensity of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 was pH-sensitive in the affinity environment because of the protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups. Incubation time is another key factor that must be investigated. Figure S6 showed that the fluorescence quenching effect swiftly increased when the incubation time was varied from 2 to 10 min in 2-min increments and finally reached equilibrium when the incubation time ranged from 10 to 20 min. Thus, the affinity sites of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 were saturated in 10 min. Therefore, MIP@CQDs@SiO2 showed the optimal fluorescence quenching performance during the recognition experiment with a solvent pH of 12 and an incubation time of 10 min.

Adsorption ability was studied by mixing 300 μL MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2 (0.5 mg/mL) with 100 μL MC-LR of different concentrations (0–2000 μg/L). Then, the mixture was oscillated by shaking table for 10 min at room temperature. After centrifugation, the supernatant was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 239 nm. The adsorption ability (Q, mg/g) of MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2 was calculated as
Q =
Δ C Â · V Â · M
m
(2), where ΔC (μg/L) is the difference between the total concentration of MC-LR and the concentration of MC-LR in the supernatant, V (L) represents the solution volume, m (mg) is the mass of each MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2, and M is the molecular weight of MC-LR. As shown in Figure S7, the adsorption ability of MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2 increased with the MC-LR concentration, and saturation adsorption could be observed at 1200 μg/L MC-LR, resulting in an adsorption ability of 3.23 mg/g. Further, the adsorption ability of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 was stronger than that of NIP@CQDs@SiO2. Thus, MIP@CQDs@SiO2 exhibited good adsorption ability toward MC-LR.

The fluorescence quenching effect of MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2 was investigated via a recognition experiment by introducing different concentrations (0, 1, 25, 50, 75, 100, 250, 500, 750, and 1000 μg/L) of MC-LR under the optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence intensity of MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2 gradually decreased with the increasing MC-LR concentration when excited at 360 nm. Regardless, the fluorescence intensity of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 considerably declined, whereas that of NIP@CQDs@SiO2 remained almost unchanged. The Stern–Volmer equation was used to obtain the imprinting factor (IF) for evaluating the recognition and sensing properties. The Stern–Volmer equation is defined as
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
(3), where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2 in the absence and presence of MC-LR, respectively, and KSV represents the Stern–Volmer quenching constant. As observed in Fig. 3, a linear relation exists between the F0/F of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 and NIP@CQDs@SiO2 and the concentration of MC-LR with correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9934 and 0.9844, respectively. Therefore, based on the curve slope of the linear response, the
K
S V
values were calculated to be 0.9114 and 0.1821 for MIP@CQDs@SiO2 and NIP@CQDs@SiO2, respectively. The imprinting factor (IF) was determined by considering KSV,MIP/KSV,NIP to be 5.00 under the optimal conditions. In addition, the detection limit of this probe was as low as 0.0093 μg/L (S/N = 3), resulting in an excellent recognition and quenching response of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 for MC-LR compared with that of NIP@CQDs@SiO2.

The fluorescence stability of MIP@CQDs@SiO2 was investigated at different times. As shown in Figure S8, the fluorescence intensity was monitored every 10 min. Only a slight decrease of 7.5% could be observed with continuous exposure to UV excitation for 200 min, resulting in short-term strong photostability. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity remained constant when MIP@CQDs@SiO2 was stored at 4 °C in darkness for 20 d, indicating excellent stability over a long period of time.

A recognition experiment was conducted with MC-LR and its analogues to study the specific selectivity of MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2. Based on Fig. 4 , MIP@CQDs@SiO2 exhibited stronger fluorescence quenching on MC-LR than NIP@CQDs@SiO2. Further, no fluorescence quenching of MIP/NIP@CQDs@SiO2 could be observed in the analogues, suggesting that MIP@CQDs@SiO2 showed excellent specific selectivity for MC-LR. Thus, MIP@CQDs@SiO2 exhibited good ability to specifically absorb MC-LR and achieved excellent fluorescence quenching effect.

The mechanism of quenching of the MIP@CQDs@SiO2 fluorescence sensor by MC-LR was ambiguous. Typically, a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism [55–57] can be considered to explain the quenching of CQDs caused by MC-LR. The quenching effect could be mainly attributed to the electron transfer between the –NH– of CQDs and the C = O of MC-LR on the frontier orbital. Because of the stronger electron adsorption ability, C = O would serve as an electron acceptor, whereas –NH– would serve as the donor. In particular, upon UV excitation, the electrons on –NH– would transfer from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Subsequently, hydrogen bonds would be formed because of the recombination between MC-LR and APTES to reduce the total energy of the MIP system, making the LUMO energy level on C = O lower than that on –NH–. Therefore, the electrons of C = O would first return to the HOMO to occupy the molecular orbital originally corresponding to –NH–, resulting in an incomplete electron transition on –NH–. Finally, the CQDs would suffer from quenching. However, the fluorescence resonance energy-transfer mechanism cannot be considered to explain the fluorescence quenching effect because the MC-LR adsorption spectrum and CQDs emission spectrum did not overlap in the appropriate wavelength range.

In this study, a novel MIP@CQDs@SiO2 fluorescence sensor with excellent selectivity toward MC-LR was constructed, and a rapid detection method that could specifically absorb MC-LR and cause the fluorescence-quenching effect in a complex matrix was established. Effective detection of MC-LR in complex water samples was realized because of various advantages of the fluorescence sensor, including high specific selectivity, sensitivity, stability, and quick response. An innovative and alternative segment template method is proposed herein, which may provide new opportunities for the detection of other substances with complex structures.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31501554), Guangdong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2020A1515010517), and Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Planning Project of China (2017A020208059).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105798.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
